_title: Your nervous system remembers what your mind forgot
_raw_content: The body keeps a ledger the mind never agreed to audit. Every flinch, every avoidance, every time you go silent mid-sentence — that is old data running live code. You don't need to remember the event. Your nervous system already has it on repeat. Healing isn't recall. It's finally letting the body finish what it started.
slides: When your body flinches before your mind even registers danger
When you shut down mid-conversation and don't know why
When 'I'm fine' leaves your mouth while your chest is on fire
When you rehearse a boundary 47 times and still say nothing
When rest feels more threatening than burnout
When someone is genuinely kind and your first instinct is suspicion
When you realize your entire personality was a survival response
caption:
Your mind moved on years ago.

Your nervous system is still in the room where it happened.

That tightness in your throat when you try to speak up — that's not weakness. That's a program still running from the first time your voice wasn't safe.

The flinch before the thought. The shutdown before the feeling. The smile that arrives faster than the truth.

These aren't flaws. They're timestamps.

Your body archived every moment your mind was forced to skip. And it will keep replaying them — not to punish you, but because it never got the signal that it's over.

Healing isn't remembering harder. It's giving your nervous system the ending it never received.

You don't need to go back to the story. You need to land in the body that survived it.
